docs.title: AIBA
docs.text: Autonomous internet browsing agent. You define the goal. It handles the rest.

docs.title: What It Does
docs.text:
It thinks, plans, discovers, distributes, searches, verifies, and synthesizes — autonomously.

It uses swarm intelligence to handle complex objectives by breaking them into smaller tasks, executing them in parallel, and weaving the results back together.

The scope of what it can do is tied to your imagination

docs.title: Some Use Cases
docs.text:
• Monitor changes across sites and surfaces you care about — on a schedule, without lifting a finger.
  • Research across dozens of sources simultaneously, cross-verify every finding, and walk away with a single synthesized report.
  • Run QA workflows against live web applications — forms, flows, visual regressions — automatically.
  • Perform personal brand threat intelligence — scheduled scans for exposure, mentions, and impersonation across platforms.
  • Track competitors over time — pricing shifts, positioning changes, feature launches, public signals.
  • Extract structured intelligence from surfaces that resist automation.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
1. Choose a mode — a single agent for depth, or a swarm for breadth.
  2. Describe your goal.
  3. AIBA plans an approach, executes it, adapts as it learns, and delivers the result.
  4. The conversation stays open. Refine. Pivot. Dig deeper.
  5. Save your session, or schedule the whole thing to run again without you.

docs.title: AIBA-beats (It Runs While You Don't)
docs.text: Beats are scheduled autonomous runs. Configure a beat once. AIBA wakes up, does the work, logs everything, and sends you the report.

docs.title: Built On
docs.text: Google Gemini · Playwright · pydantic-ai · logfire

docs.title: Start Here
docs.text: Getting Started — follow the guide and you'll be running in under five minutes.

docs.title: Contributing to AIBA
docs.text: First off — thank you for considering a contribution. AIBA aims to be a high-quality, production-grade autonomous agent, and every improvement counts.

docs.title: Before You Start
docs.text:
Read the Developer Guidelines & Code Rules (rules.md in the project root). This is the single source of truth for how the codebase is built and maintained. The highlights:

Principle What it means Product-Grade Quality Every line should feel production-ready. No shortcuts. Simplicity > Cleverness Prefer clear, explicit logic over dense one-liners. Single Responsibility One function, one job. Smart Abstraction Don't abstract unless the logic is shared by multiple callers. Minimalist Footprint Write what's needed — no speculative features. Zero-Bug Tolerance Think through edge cases before you write code. Concise Documentation Comment the why, not the what. Keep it brief. Use UV Build, test, lint, and type-check through uv run.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text: Tool Version Why Python 3.13+ The runtime. uv latest Package & environment manager. Node.js 18+ Required by Playwright MCP. npm 8+ Ships with Node.

docs.title: 1. Clone & install
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/hamza-mughal1/AIBA
cd AIBA
uv sync --dev

uv sync --dev installs both runtime and development dependencies (pytest, ruff, pyright, etc.).

docs.title: 2. Install the browser
docs.text: uv run playwright install chromium

docs.title: 3. Create your environment file
docs.text:
cp .env.example .env

Open .env and set the one required field:

GEMINI_API_KEY=AIza...your-key-here
Do I need a Gemini key to contribute?

If you're only modifying non-agent code (tests, docs, configuration, CI), you can skip the API key. Tests that call the LLM use TestModel mocks and never make real API requests.

docs.title: The Contribution Workflow
docs.text:
graph LR
    A[Clone & install] --> B[Create branch]
    B --> C[Run tests<br/>to confirm baseline]
    C --> D[Make changes]
    D --> E[Run ruff & pyright]
    E --> F[Run tests]
    F --> G{PASS?}
    G -->|Yes| H[Push & create MR]
    G -->|No| D

docs.title: 1. Create a branch
docs.text:
git checkout -b feat/my-feature

Use a descriptive prefix:

Prefix When feat/ New feature fix/ Bug fix docs/ Documentation refactor/ Code restructuring test/ Adding or improving tests chore/ Tooling, CI, dependencies

docs.title: 2. Confirm the baseline
docs.text:
Before making any changes, verify everything passes:

uv run pytest tests/unit/ -q
# Expected: all tests passed!

uv run ruff check
# Expected: All checks passed!

uv run pyright
# Expected: 0 errors, 0 warnings

docs.title: 3. Make your changes
docs.text:
Follow the rules in rules.md — especially:

  • Single Responsibility: Don't stuff multiple concerns into one function.
  • Minimalist Footprint: Add exactly what the feature needs, nothing more.
  • Explicit Logic: Write code that's easy to scan and understand.
  • No Truncation: When editing files, provide complete contents — no // ... placeholders.

If you're adding or modifying any pydantic-ai, playwright, or google-genai logic, consult the latest official documentation for those libraries to avoid deprecated patterns.

docs.title: 4. Run quality checks again
docs.text:
# Full test suite with coverage
uv run pytest tests/unit/ --cov=src --cov-report=term-missing -q

# Linting
uv run ruff check

# Type checking
uv run pyright

The project maintains 100% coverage and zero lint/type errors. Your contribution should preserve both.

docs.title: 5. Push and create a Merge Request
docs.text:
git push -u origin feat/my-feature

Then open a Merge Request on GitHub. In the description, include:

  • What the change does.
  • Why it's needed.
  • How you verified it (tests run, lint clean, type-check passed).

docs.title: Code Review Expectations
docs.text:
All contributions go through code review. Reviewers will check for:

  • Correctness and edge-case coverage.
  • Adherence to rules.md principles.
  • Test coverage (new code should be tested).
  • No regressions (existing tests must pass).
  • Clean lint and type-check results.

Don't take feedback personally — the goal is a shared codebase we're all proud of.

docs.title: Need Help?
docs.text: Open a Discussion or an Issue. We're happy to help you get started.
